raw_case:
Rasmussen aneurysm: A hidden hazard
Tuberculosis is endemic in Pakistan and other countries in South Asia despite various treatment programs being introduced for its elimination. It leads to a number of serious consequences which involve different organ systems of the body and Rasmussen Aneurysm is one such serious complication. It is the pseudo-aneurysmal dilatation of the pulmonary artery adjacent to a tubercular cavity and manifests as massive hemoptysis, like in the case of our patient. It is important to note that our patient was not diagnosed with TB and also denied any common symptoms and contact with persons with TB. This signifies that the progression to Rasmussen Aneurysm is not linear and anyone with TB may present with life-threatening hemoptysis, therefore this case report proves to be essential in raising awareness of such instances. This further highlights the need for nationwide screening programs and designated management plans.1

Background

Tuberculosis (TB) is highly epidemic in Pakistan, with 69000 TB deaths and 410000 incident cases every year. It has the sixth highest burden in the world which often results in one or more repercussions [1]. These include infection of the bones, spine, brain and spinal cord, lymph glands and other parts of the body. While such incidences are often reported, a forgotten complication is Rasmussen aneurysm that is a serious consequence of pulmonary tuberculosis which may present even after the treatment has been completed [2]. Rasmussen aneurysm is an inflammatory pseudo-aneurysmal dilatation of pulmonary artery adjacent to a tubercular cavity. This occurs due to progressive weakening of the arterial wall and replacement of adventitia and media with granulation tissue which is gradually replaced by fibrin thereby thinning the arterial wall [3]. It is usually distributed peripherally and beyond the branches of the main pulmonary artery and clinically presents as massive hemoptysis with an associated mortality ranging from 5% to 25 %. It is diagnosed by contrast computed tomography, which demonstrates focal enhancement within the pulmonary aneurysm.Our aim with this case report is to describe the case of a patient with Rasmussen aneurysm in a low-middle income country which may easily go unnoticed. We will further evaluate the radiographic findings and track the treatment and recovery of this patient in order to understand any complications that may arise.2

Case presentation

A 47 year old male patient arrived to the emergency department on 13th of June 2024 with complaint of recurrent hemoptysis. He had a 14 year history of diabetes for which he took oral hypoglycemic at home and was a former smoker with 6–10 pack years. For the past two months he developed occasional undocumented fevers and intermittent productive cough without hemoptysis. He then developed shortness of breath of for two days and then on 3rd June 2024, he reported 3 episodes of hematemesis, 2 episodes of hemoptysis and 1 episode of melena. Due to these active complaints he went to a tertiary care hospital in Karachi on 5th June 2024 from where he was referred for bronchial artery embolization for which he came to the AKUH emergency department. On arrival his blood pressure was 111/19, pulse was 87, respiratory rate was 30 and oxygen saturation was 99 %. On examination of the chest, there were left-sided crepitus and normal heart sounds. His abdomen was soft and non-tender alongside a Glasgow coma scale of 15 and crusted blood in the oral cavity. On inquiry, the patient denied any history of tuberculosis and/or contact with persons with tuberculosis.3

Imaging

A chest CT with contrast was done which identified a large, irregularly thick walled cavity in left upper lobe with atelectasis of the rest of the upper lobe parenchyma. The cavity measured 68 × 73 mm in anteroposterior and transverse dimension with an ill-defined soft tissue alongside an overlying air crescent seen placed within this cavity measuring 51 x 66 mm. In the arterial phase, a small well-defined focal area of contrast is also seen within this cavity which is likely arising from the left upper lobe pulmonary artery measuring 15 x 15 × 18 mm in anteroposterior, transverse and craniocaudal dimensions representing a focal aneurysm (Fig. 1). Left pulmonary artery was seen as small in caliber and significantly thinned out.In the right lung, small thick-walled cavities are also seen scattered throughout the lung with the largest cavity in the anterior segment of right upper lobe which measured 20 × 13 mm in size. Fibrotic changes in bilateral apices, multiple prominent mediastinal lymph nodes effusion were also identified. The CT findings indicated a chronic bilateral pulmonary granulomatous infection, most likely tuberculosis and Rasmussen aneurysm within the large cavity in the left upper lobe.4

Plan/treatment

According to the suggestive findings of tuberculosis on the CT imaging, the patient was started on anti-tuberculosis medication. Sputum was also sent for Xpert MTB/RIF which detect Mycobacterium Tuberculosis and AFB smear reported numerous acid fast bacilli. He was then referred to vascular and interventional radiology (VIR) immediately due to risk of life-threatening hemoptysis which could have resulted as a consequence of Rasmussen aneurysm rupture. On the same day of admission, June 13, 2024, the patient was taken for glue embolization of the pseudo-aneurysm. After standard preparation of the groin, a 4 Fr venous access sheath was placed after the insertion of a 4F pigtail catheter into the left pulmonary artery. During the angiogram, the aneurysm was seen arising from the segmental branch of the left main pulmonary artery which was embolized using Histoacryl. After the procedure, vascular access sheath was removed and hemostasis was achieved through manual compression. A chest x-ray was done to visualize the placement of the stent after the procedure (Fig. 2).5

Follow-up

A follow-up chest x-ray was done on 13th September 2024. In comparison to the previous radiograph, there is mild interval improvement in the airspace opacities which were visualized in the left lung field. High density material is seen in the left upper zone which is suggestive of embolized glue material. This finding is consistent with history of angioembolization (Fig. 3).6

Discussion

Due to a high burden of tuberculosis in Pakistan, the population has developed multi-drug resistance, which was specifically reported in Sindh [4]. This signifies that transmission of tuberculosis is rampant in low and middle income countries which have inadequate treatment facilities and guidance that may encourage proper treatment regimens.It is important to note that our patient presented to the emergency department without a confirmed diagnosis of tuberculosis and further denied any contacts with persons with TB. Therefore, it is essential to intensify TB awareness programs alongside infection control measures and high quality MDR-TB detection strategies [5]. TB subsequently can lead to a variety of complications amongst which Rasmussen aneurysm is rare therefore often overlooked by physicians. However, in cases of massive hemoptysis, as was the case with our patient, Rasmussen aneurysm must be ruled out through a chest CT and pulmonary angiogram [6].Rasmussen aneurysm arise from the segmental pulmonary arteries and occur in conjunction with cavitary TB which is mostly seen in patients who are latent carries [7]. In our patient, the cavitation that resulted due to TB was particularly large in size, measuring 68 × 73 mm in anteroposterior and transverse dimensions. Once Rasmussen aneurysm is suspected, treatment plan must immediately be initiated. After analyzing the chest CT, our patient was immediately taken for pulmonary angiogram and glue embolization on the same day of admission because of risk of massive bleeding. In patients whom the glue embolization does not prove to be successful, lobectomy is usually the second-line of treatment [8]. However, overall mortality with a surgical approach for hemoptysis is significantly high. Our patient was discharged with anti-tuberculosis medication and on follow-up, was in good health.7

Conclusion

In resource limited countries like Pakistan where diagnosis and treatment of infectious diseases is limited, it is important to understand the presentation of TB. Besides the usual signs and symptoms, if a patient presents with massive hemoptysis in a region with high TB incidence,Rasmussen aneurysm can be suspected and considering its time-sensitive nature, appropriate interventions can be made. Accurate diagnosis largely depends on the patient's history and chest CT, after which timely embolization of the pulmonary artery can prove to be life-saving.

CRediT authorship contribution statement

Duaa Amir: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Hina Inam: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Muhammad Mehdi Irfani: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

Declaration of competing interest

The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 47-year-old male, former smoker (6–10 pack years), history of diabetes (14 years, managed with oral hypoglycemic agents).
- **Chief Complaint:** Recurrent hemoptysis.
- **History of Present Illness:** Two months of undocumented fevers, intermittent productive cough without hemoptysis, and two days of worsening shortness of breath. Presented with multiple episodes of hemoptysis, hematemesis, and melena.
- **Past Medical History:** Diabetes Mellitus, no prior TB diagnosis.
- **Personal History:** Former smoker; denies TB exposure or contact.
- **Family History:** None reported.
- **Physical Examination:**
  - Vitals: BP 111/19, pulse 87, RR 30, SpO2 99%.
  - Findings: Left-sided chest crepitus, normal heart sounds, crusted blood in the oral cavity, soft and non-tender abdomen.
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - Chest CT: Large thick-walled cavity in the left upper lobe with atelectasis, fibrotic changes, and evidence of a focal aneurysm within the cavity. Smaller thick-walled cavities in the right lung.
  - Lab Results: Positive Xpert MTB/RIF for Mycobacterium tuberculosis, numerous acid-fast bacilli on AFB smear.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Considerations:**
   - The patient presenting with massive hemoptysis prompted consideration of pulmonary sources, including tuberculosis-related complications (e.g., cavitary tuberculosis, aspergilloma, or Rasmussen aneurysm), malignancy, or vascular etiologies such as arteriovenous malformations or pseudo-aneurysm.
2. **Key Findings:**
   - CT imaging confirmed cavitary changes consistent with chronic granulomatous infection (likely tuberculosis) and identified a pseudo-aneurysm (Rasmussen aneurysm) in the left upper lobe.
   - Diagnostics confirmed Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection, ruling out other possible etiologies of granulomatous lung disease (e.g., fungal infections or non-tuberculous mycobacteria).
   - Absence of weight loss, night sweats, or constitutional symptoms reduced primary consideration for malignancy.
   - An angiogram confirmed the source of hemoptysis as a segmental left pulmonary artery pseudo-aneurysm, excluding other vascular causes like arteriovenous malformations.
3. **Exclusion and Confirmation of Diagnosis:**
   - Rasmussen aneurysm was confirmed based on pseudo-aneurysm localization adjacent to the tuberculous cavity and angiographic findings. Sudden massive hemoptysis further solidified this as the primary etiology.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Rasmussen aneurysm secondary to cavitary pulmonary tuberculosis.

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> **Analyze Chief Complaint and History of Present Illness:** The patient presents with recurrent hemoptysis, which is the most alarming symptom and the primary driver for seeking medical attention.  The history of two months of undocumented fevers and intermittent productive cough suggests an underlying infectious or inflammatory process. The worsening shortness of breath in the last two days indicates respiratory compromise. Hematemesis and melena, while potentially concerning for gastrointestinal bleeding, in the context of hemoptysis, are more likely due to swallowed blood from the respiratory tract.

<step 2> **Evaluate Past Medical and Personal History:**  The patient's history of diabetes mellitus is significant as it increases susceptibility to infections, including tuberculosis. Being a former smoker, even with a relatively low pack-year history, increases the risk for pulmonary diseases.  The denial of prior TB diagnosis or exposure doesn't exclude TB, as exposure can be asymptomatic or forgotten, and reactivation of latent TB is possible, especially in diabetic patients.

<step 3> **Assess Physical Examination Findings:**  The elevated respiratory rate (30 breaths per minute) and shortness of breath (dyspnea) indicate respiratory distress.  While SpO2 is reported as 99%, this might be misleading without knowing if supplemental oxygen was administered or if the measurement is accurate in the context of respiratory distress and potential shunting due to lung pathology. Left-sided chest crepitus strongly suggests lung pathology, such as pneumonia, bronchitis, or fluid in the lungs. Crusted blood in the oral cavity directly supports the chief complaint of hemoptysis. Normal heart sounds are less concerning for primary cardiac causes of respiratory distress.

<step 4> **Interpret Chest CT Findings:** The chest CT is highly revealing.  A large thick-walled cavity in the left upper lobe is a classic finding in pulmonary tuberculosis. Atelectasis and fibrotic changes indicate chronicity and tissue destruction. The presence of a focal aneurysm within the cavity is extremely significant and strongly suggests a Rasmussen aneurysm, a known complication of cavitary tuberculosis and a major cause of life-threatening hemoptysis.  Smaller thick-walled cavities in the right lung indicate bilateral lung involvement, further supporting a disseminated infectious process like tuberculosis.

<step 5> **Interpret Lab Results:** The positive Xpert MTB/RIF result is a rapid and highly specific test for *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*. This definitively confirms the presence of tuberculosis. The report of "numerous acid-fast bacilli on AFB smear" further corroborates the diagnosis of active tuberculosis and indicates a high bacterial load.

<step 6> **Synthesize Findings and Formulate Diagnosis:**  Combining the clinical presentation (hemoptysis, cough, fever, shortness of breath), risk factors (diabetes, former smoker), suggestive chest CT findings (cavities, aneurysm), and definitive laboratory confirmation (positive Xpert MTB/RIF and AFB smear), the diagnosis of pulmonary tuberculosis is unequivocally established. The Rasmussen aneurysm identified on CT explains the hemoptysis and represents a serious complication of the tuberculosis. The hematemesis and melena are most likely due to swallowed blood from the hemoptysis.

<step 7> **Final Diagnostic Conclusion:** Based on the comprehensive evaluation, the final diagnosis is active pulmonary tuberculosis complicated by a Rasmussen aneurysm, which is the likely source of the patient's hemoptysis.  The diabetes mellitus is a significant comorbidity that likely contributed to the development and severity of the tuberculosis.

### Answer:
Pulmonary Tuberculosis with Rasmussen Aneurysm and Hemoptysis
